meta.title: LuauX
meta.tagline: The dependency-ordered path to Luau mastery
meta.profile: Intermediate Roblox dev with scattered gaps. Solid on basic syntax; wants deep mastery of replication, parallel Luau / actors, metatables, OOP, and the memory model — with a fast refresh of the basics.

tracks.name: Orientation & Mental Model
tracks.blurb: The "where does my code live and run" model. Everything else assumes it.

items.c: One published place runs simultaneously on a server and many clients — the platform's core mental model.
items.prt: Entry point — nothing precedes it.

items.c: Luau scripts attach to the DataModel and run inside the engine, not as standalone programs.
items.prt: Needs the platform model from the page above.

items.c: The game is a live tree of Instances rooted at `game`, organized into service containers.
items.prt: "Scripts attach to something" — you need the tree they attach to.

items.c: Engine features are reached through singleton Services fetched with GetService.
items.prt: Services are nodes inside the DataModel.

items.c: Where a script sits decides whether and where it runs — server, client, or not at all.
items.prt: You must know the DataModel containers before their run-context matters.

tracks.name: Language Core (Luau)
tracks.blurb: A fast refresh of syntax, then the two pages that unlock OOP later.

items.c: Luau is Roblox's gradually-typed Lua 5.1 descendant.
items.prt: Track entry point.

items.c: Names bind to values; `local` vs global.
items.prt: You can't use any data type without a variable to hold it.

items.c: `--` and `--[[ ]]` are ignored at parse time.
items.prt: Trivial; placed early for completeness, no real dependency.

items.c: `nil` means absence, and assigning `nil` removes a value.
items.prt: "A variable can hold nothing" extends Variables.

items.c: `true`/`false` and truthiness — only `nil` and `false` are falsy.
items.prt: No dependency between the three primitive-type pages.

items.c: All numbers are 64-bit doubles.
items.prt: Parallel with booleans/strings.

items.c: Immutable character sequences and the string library.
items.prt: Parallel with booleans/numbers.

items.c: Arithmetic, relational, and logical operators, plus the `..` concatenation operator.
items.prt: Operators act on the primitive types above.

items.c: Luau auto-converts between strings and numbers in arithmetic and concatenation.
items.prt: Coercion is defined in terms of the operators.

items.c: Blocks define visibility; lexical scoping and closures.
items.prt: Scope is meaningless without variables.

items.c: `if`/`while`/`for`/`repeat` branching and looping.
items.prt: Conditions are boolean expressions (needs Booleans + Operators).

items.c: Reusable callable blocks with parameters and closures over scope.
items.prt: Function bodies contain control structures and rely on Scope.

items.c: Functions can return and accept multiple values (tuples).
items.prt: A tuple is a function's multi-return — needs Functions.

items.c: The single structured type: arrays, dictionaries, and nesting.
items.prt: Constructors and iteration use functions and loops already covered.

items.c: Roblox's fixed `Enum` datatype, e.g. `Enum.KeyCode`.
items.prt: Enums are accessed like tables.

items.c: Optional type annotations and inference for safer code.
items.prt: You annotate the values/functions/tables from all pages above. (Typed Luau kills exactly the "scattered gaps" bugs.)

items.c: Metatables and metamethods (`__index`, `__newindex`, operator overloads) let tables emulate classes.
items.prt: A metatable IS a table attached to a table — hard-requires Tables.

items.c: Opaque C/C++ values — most Roblox datatypes like `Vector3` are userdata.
items.prt: Contrasts with Tables; matters once you hit identity/`==` edge cases.

items.c: A FIFO structure implemented over a table.
items.prt: Built on Tables. Parallel with Stacks.

items.c: A LIFO structure implemented over a table.
items.prt: Built on Tables. Parallel with Queues.

items.c: A translation table for developers coming from C#.
items.prt: Lookup-only, no path position.

items.c: `--!native` compiles hot Luau to machine code.
items.prt: An optimization — only read after you can profile (Track 7).

tracks.name: Instances, Events, Scheduling & Input
tracks.blurb: How running code observes and reacts to the live DataModel.

items.c: Read and write Instance Properties and define your own custom Attributes from script.
items.prt: Needs the DataModel (Track 0) and variables (Track 1).

items.c: Instances expose signals you `Connect` callbacks to — the event-driven model.
items.prt: Event handlers are functions (Track 1) bound to Instances (Track 0).

items.c: `task.wait/spawn/defer/delay` and how code is scheduled across frames.
items.prt: Scheduling resumes the callbacks introduced in Events.

items.c: `BindableEvent`/`BindableFunction` for same-side (server↔server or client↔client) custom signals.
items.prt: A specialization of the generic event model.

items.c: The debounce / cooldown pattern that stops an event firing its handler too often.
items.prt: A pattern applied to event connections.

items.c: Deferred event behavior defers handlers to resumption points, changing ordering and reentrancy.
items.prt: You must understand normal event firing and the scheduler first.

items.c: Input arrives as engine events on the client.
items.prt: Input is an events topic — needs the Track 2 events model.

items.c: The modern device-agnostic system that abstracts key/pad/touch into named actions.
items.prt: Builds on the input overview. Prefer this over raw-device pages for new work.

items.c: Raw `UserInputService`/`ContextActionService` keyboard and mouse handling.
items.prt: Per-device; parallel with gamepad/mobile.

items.c: Gamepad button and thumbstick specifics.
items.prt: Parallel with mouse/mobile.

items.c: Touch and on-screen control specifics.
items.prt: Parallel with mouse/gamepad.

tracks.name: Code Architecture, Modules & OOP
tracks.blurb: Turning scattered scripts into reusable, structured systems.

items.c: `ModuleScript`s return a value once and are `require`d to share code and state.
items.prt: Modules return tables/functions (Track 1) and live in the DataModel (Track 0).

items.c: The `Class.__index = Class; setmetatable(obj, Class)` convention the docs assume but never teach in one place.
items.prt: Combines Metatables + ModuleScripts. → see Beyond the Docs §G1.

items.c: Sandbox capabilities restrict what a script — especially required third-party code — is allowed to do.
items.prt: Only meaningful once you `require` external modules.

tracks.name: Client–Server, Replication & Security
tracks.blurb: The single biggest source of "scattered gaps" for intermediate devs.

items.c: The server and each client run separate Luau VMs; server changes replicate down and clients cannot be trusted.
items.prt: Requires the run-locations model (Track 0).

items.c: `RemoteEvent`/`RemoteFunction` are the only sanctioned way to communicate across the client–server boundary.
items.prt: Remotes cross the boundary defined in Client–server; a specialization of Events.

items.c: `Player`/`UserId` identity and domain-scoped IDs.
items.prt: Server logic keys on player identity; could sit in Track 0, placed by remotes.

items.c: `leaderstats` is a folder whose children auto-replicate to the player-list UI.
items.prt: A concrete replication example — needs Client–server.

items.c: Never trust the client; validate every remote argument on the server.
items.prt: Directly secures the Remote mechanism.

items.c: What assets and values are visible to a client, and how to keep secrets server-only.
items.prt: Extends the boundary concept to confidentiality.

items.c: Design systems so the server owns truth and cheating is structurally impossible.
items.prt: A generalization of the boundary rules.

items.c: A concrete catalog of cheat-mitigation tactics.
items.prt: Applies defensive design; parallel with the two below.

items.c: Detect and consequence anomalous behavior on the server.
items.prt: Parallel with the other tactic pages.

items.c: Attack vectors from imported models and plugins.
items.prt: Ties to Capabilities (Track 3); parallel with the other tactic pages.

items.c: The formal model: the server is authoritative over state.
items.prt: Names and structures the principle the security pages applied ad hoc.

items.c: Patterns to keep authority server-side without ruining responsiveness.
items.prt: Techniques for the model above.

items.c: All user-generated text must pass through `TextService:FilterStringAsync` for safety and compliance.
items.prt: A server-side trust/validation rule — belongs after the boundary pages.

items.c: `TeleportService` moves players between places/servers, carrying data.
items.prt: Teleport data crosses a trust boundary — needs the security mindset.

tracks.name: 3D Workspace & Physics Scripting
tracks.blurb: Scripting against the spatial and physics side of the engine.

items.c: The `Workspace` is the rendered, physically simulated subtree of the DataModel.
items.prt: Specializes the DataModel (Track 0).

items.c: `CFrame` encodes position AND orientation — the math for moving and rotating parts.
items.prt: The foundational spatial datatype the rest of this track uses.

items.c: Cast rays to detect what's in the world: hit detection, line-of-sight.
items.prt: Rays are built from position+direction vectors; CFrame is the closest forcing prerequisite (no standalone Vector3 page).

items.c: Collision groups and `CanCollide`/`Touched` control what physically interacts.
items.prt: A spatial-query topic parallel with raycasting.

items.c: Script the client `Camera` through its `CFrame` and camera modes.
items.prt: The camera is positioned with CFrames; client-only (needs Track 4 boundary intuition).

items.c: Streaming loads parts of the Workspace on demand, so the client may not have every Instance.
items.prt: Changes the replication assumptions from Track 4 — read after it.

items.c: Designing scripts that tolerate streamed-out instances.
items.prt: Techniques for the streaming model above.

items.c: Connected parts form a single simulated assembly.
items.prt: Needs the Workspace/parts model; parallel with the sleep system.

items.c: Idle assemblies "sleep" to save simulation cost.
items.prt: Defined in terms of assemblies; parallel with assemblies.

items.c: Which machine — server or a client — simulates a given assembly.
items.prt: Ownership is of an assembly.

items.c: The security framing of ownership: why client-owned physics can be exploited.
items.prt: Needs both physics ownership and the Track 4 trust model.

items.c: `PathfindingService` computes walkable paths through the Workspace.
items.prt: Operates on Workspace geometry + raycasting concepts.

tracks.name: Data Persistence & Cross-Server Services
tracks.blurb: Saving state and coordinating across server instances.

items.c: Durable (DataStore) vs ephemeral/shared (MemoryStore) storage, and when to pick each.
items.prt: The decision frames every page below; needs server authority (Track 4).

items.c: `DataStoreService` for persistent per-key data across sessions.
items.prt: The concrete service the comparison page contrasted.

items.c: Session locking, retries, and avoiding data loss or duplication.
items.prt: Patterns layered on the basic API.

items.c: A full player-data and purchase-handling system.
items.prt: Integrates data stores with player identity (Track 4) — the capstone example.

items.c: Version history, key listing, and cache semantics.
items.prt: Advanced facets of the same API.

items.c: The quota / throttle / error-code table.
items.prt: Lookup-only.

items.c: Handling data-deletion (RTBF) compliance requests.
items.prt: A policy layer on stored data.

items.c: Fast, shared, short-lived data across all servers.
items.prt: The MemoryStore half of the comparison page.

items.c: An ordered shared map, e.g. for global leaderboards.
items.prt: Parallel with hash-map and queue.

items.c: A keyed shared map.
items.prt: Parallel with sorted-map and queue.

items.c: A shared cross-server queue, e.g. for matchmaking.
items.prt: Parallel with the other MemoryStore structures.

items.c: Design guidance for MemoryStore data structures.
items.prt: Lookup.

items.c: Partition limits and data distribution.
items.prt: Lookup.

items.c: `MessagingService` publishes and subscribes events between live servers.
items.prt: Cross-server coordination; needs the multi-server model.

items.c: `HttpService` calls external web APIs from the server.
items.prt: Server-only outbound I/O; independent of the storage pages.

items.c: Store API keys and secrets out of source.
items.prt: Secrets exist to feed HttpService — read right after it.

tracks.name: Performance, Parallel Luau & the Memory Model
tracks.blurb: The deep-mastery zone: actors, profiling, and memory.

items.c: The measure-before-you-optimize loop and what the frame budget is.
items.prt: Needs a working model of scripts/events/replication (Tracks 0–5).

items.c: Map symptoms to a likely cause: CPU vs memory vs network vs render.
items.prt: Step 1 of the loop above.

items.c: Live stats and diagnostics to watch.
items.prt: How to read what Identify flagged; parallel with the profiler pages.

items.c: Concrete fixes, including reducing per-frame work and garbage / memory churn.
items.prt: The fix step; also the densest single source on the memory model.

items.c: Architecting for performance up front.
items.prt: Generalizes the fixes once you've done them once.

items.c: Analyze a place's asset and instance cost.
items.prt: Tooling-flavored; read when a specific place is heavy.

items.c: Actors isolate state, code runs in RunService parallel phases, and threads communicate only via SharedTable / messaging.
items.prt: Requires the event/scheduler model (Track 2) AND a real perf reason — read only after you can profile.

items.c: Read the live memory breakdown to find leaks: untracked instances, growing tables, dangling connections.
items.prt: The concrete tool for the memory model from Improve.

items.c: Per-function CPU attribution.
items.prt: The tool that backs Identify/Improve for CPU.

tracks.name: Tooling, Debugging & Workflow
tracks.blurb: Usable throughout — placed last only because nothing depends on it conceptually. Dip in from day one.

items.c: The in-Studio editor, autocomplete, and script analysis.
items.prt: Workflow — usable from day one.

items.c: The Output window and `print`/`warn`/`error`.
items.prt: Pair with the Script Editor; no hard order.

items.c: Breakpoints, watches, and the call stack.
items.prt: Debugging acts on running scripts — most useful after Tracks 1–2.

items.c: Play / Run / Team Test and how each simulates client vs server.
items.prt: The modes only make sense with the client–server model (Track 4).

items.c: The in-experience runtime console, including on live servers.
items.prt: A runtime view of the same logs and stats.

items.c: Edit Luau on local disk through an external editor.
items.prt: A workflow over existing scripts; independent of the rest of the track.

items.c: Rojo and other third-party file-based toolchains.
items.prt: Extends the external-editor idea from Script Sync.

items.c: Studio plugins are themselves Luau scripts.
items.prt: Writing one needs the full scripting model; parallel with widgets.

items.c: Plugin UI surfaces.
items.prt: Needs Plugins; parallel with the plugins page.

tracks.name: Engine API Reference
tracks.blurb: The dictionary, not a chapter. Never read linearly.

items.c: The per-class and per-datatype API: Instance, Vector3, CFrame, RunService, every service.
items.prt: No position — jump in whenever a page names a class and you need its exact members.

gaps.title: Metatable-based OOP — the class convention
gaps.why: The docs teach Metatables and ModuleScripts separately but never the canonical class pattern, `self`, `:` vs `.`, or inheritance. This is the single most important assumed convention in real Roblox code.

body.p: A class is a table that doubles as the metatable (`__index`) of its instances. `function T:method()` is sugar for `function T.method(self)` — the colon passes the instance as `self`.

body.code:
-- Animal.lua  (a ModuleScript)
local Animal = {}
Animal.__index = Animal          -- instances fall back to Animal for methods

function Animal.new(name, sound)
    local self = setmetatable({}, Animal)
    self.name = name
    self.sound = sound
    return self
end

function Animal:speak()           -- ':' => self is the instance
    print(self.name .. " says " .. self.sound)
end

return Animal

body.p: A subclass sets its own `__index` to itself, and chains to the parent so missing methods are found one level up.

body.code:
-- Dog.lua
local Animal = require(script.Parent.Animal)

local Dog = setmetatable({}, { __index = Animal })  -- Dog inherits Animal
Dog.__index = Dog

function Dog.new(name)
    local self = Animal.new(name, "Woof")           -- call the base constructor
    return setmetatable(self, Dog)                  -- but re-type as a Dog
end

function Dog:fetch()
    print(self.name .. " fetches the ball")
end

return Dog

list: Forgetting `Class.__index = Class` → methods are `nil` on instances.
Calling a method with `.` instead of `:` → `self` is the first real argument, everything shifts.
Storing per-instance state on the class table instead of `self` → every instance shares it.

gaps.title: Client–server boundary intuition
gaps.why: The docs state the rules and list tactics, but the day-to-day instinct — "is this value even present on this side right now?" — is spread across three tracks. This is the #1 intermediate gap.

list: Server changes to the DataModel replicate DOWN to clients automatically. Client changes do NOT go up — the server never sees them unless you fire a RemoteEvent.
A LocalScript change (e.g. moving a part) is visible only on that one client and is purely cosmetic; the server's copy is unchanged.
Anything the player must not fake (currency, damage, ownership) must be decided on the SERVER. The client only ever requests.
Never send 'trusted' data through a RemoteEvent argument — the client controls every value it sends.

gaps.title: When to actually reach for Actors / Parallel Luau
gaps.why: The docs explain HOW parallel Luau works but barely WHEN it's worth the complexity. Most gameplay logic should stay serial.

list: You have a large, independent workload: hundreds of raycasts, pathfinding for many NPCs, procedural/voxel generation, mesh or terrain analysis.
The work is embarrassingly parallel — each unit of work needs no other unit's result.
A profiler (Track 7) has PROVEN this code is your CPU bottleneck.
It's ordinary gameplay/event logic — the synchronization overhead usually costs more than it saves.
The work touches shared mutable state on every step (parallel code can only READ most of the DataModel; writes must be back in serial).
You haven't profiled yet. Parallelizing on a hunch is the classic premature optimization.

body.code:
-- Inside a Script under an Actor:
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService")

RunService.PreSimulation:ConnectParallel(function()
    -- heavy, read-only work runs here across worker threads
    local result = expensiveRaycastBatch()
    task.synchronize()           -- step back into serial to safely WRITE
    applyResult(result)
end)

body.p: Rule of thumb: profile first, parallelize only a proven, embarrassingly-parallel hot path, and keep all writes inside `task.synchronize()`.

gaps.title: The memory model & object lifetime
gaps.why: There is no dedicated garbage-collection page. GC, references, connection cleanup, Destroy, and weak tables are scattered across four pages. Assemble them into one model.

body.p: Luau is garbage-collected: a table/closure/instance is collected only when nothing references it any more. A single lingering reference keeps the whole object (and everything it points to) alive.

list: Event connections you never `:Disconnect()` — the closure (and its upvalues) stays alive as long as the signal does.
Instances you `:Destroy()` but still hold in a table — Destroy parents it to nil and locks it, but your reference keeps it in memory.
Per-player tables keyed by Player that you never clear on PlayerRemoving.
Growing caches with no eviction.

body.code:
-- Clean up connections explicitly:
local conn = part.Touched:Connect(onTouch)
-- ...later...
conn:Disconnect()                 -- frees the closure

-- Destroy AND drop the reference:
model:Destroy()
model = nil

-- A weak table lets the GC reclaim values that nothing else references:
local cache = setmetatable({}, { __mode = "v" })

body.p: `Instance:Destroy()` severs the parent, disconnects the instance's own events, and locks it — but it does NOT free memory until your Luau references to it are also gone.

gaps.title: Custom Signal objects
gaps.why: Real codebases often use a lightweight custom `Signal` class instead of `BindableEvent` (no instance overhead, no argument serialization). The docs only cover the built-in `BindableEvent`.

body.p: Once you have OOP (§G1) you can build a minimal signal. This is the shape almost every community library uses:
Use a real BindableEvent only when you need cross-script communication where one side is a different Script instance; for in-module pub/sub, a plain Signal is faster and keeps argument types intact.

gaps.title: The frame loop (RunService)
gaps.why: `Heartbeat`/`PreRender`/`PreSimulation` stepping is central to movement, cameras, and parallel phases, but the docs only mention it piecemeal.

head: Event
When
Use it for
rows: right before the frame renders (client only)
camera moves, UI that must not lag a frame behind
forces/impulses you want physics to consume this step
reading the result of this step's physics

body.code:
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService")

RunService.Heartbeat:Connect(function(dt)
    -- dt = seconds since last frame; multiply by dt to stay framerate-independent
    part.Position += Vector3.new(0, 5 * dt, 0)
end)

body.p: Prefer `RunService` per-frame events over `while task.wait() do` loops for anything that should move smoothly — they're synced to the render/physics pipeline and hand you an accurate `deltaTime`.

gaps.title: Replication: measure before you optimize
gaps.why: Replication and RemoteEvent traffic can become a bottleneck, but fixed budgets and packet rules are not safe universal advice. Use the current Roblox diagnostics and a specific symptom before changing a network design.

body.p: A replication issue is a hypothesis when a device, place version, or player count shows a repeatable symptom. Compare the same action under controlled conditions, then inspect the relevant current Studio or Creator Dashboard diagnostics.

list: Firing full tables over RemoteEvents frequently. Instead, send indexes or delta IDs.
Allowing clients to replicate movement at high frequencies without server-side validation/clamping.
Replicating large quantities of workspace parts to all players. Use StreamingEnabled to load parts dynamically.
Property spamming (updating attributes or properties in tight loops causes replication queues to overflow).

body.code:
-- Bad: Sending a full array of changes every frame
task.wait(0.1)
remote:FireAllClients(currentWorldState)

-- Good: Sending only changed keys (Delta compression)
local changes = {}
for id, state in pairs(currentWorldState) do
    if state.dirty then
        changes[id] = state.value
        state.dirty = false
    end
end
if next(changes) then
    remote:FireAllClients(changes)
end

body.p: Start with the smallest data and frequency that serve the player-facing result. Do not invent a compression scheme or throttle because of a folklore number; measure, change one thing, and verify the actual symptom improves.

gaps.title: Engine interaction in hot loops
gaps.why: Repeated Instance/property work can matter in a measured hot path, but Roblox does not publish a universal per-access cost that should drive architecture. Profile a real symptom first.

body.p: Separate the game result from the implementation detail: profile the slow path, identify whether scripting, physics, rendering, memory, or networking is involved, then make the smallest testable change.

list: Avoid reading the same property repeatedly when one local snapshot expresses the same intended calculation.
Prefer a documented query or bulk API when it represents the question you actually need to ask.
Do pure value math locally where it preserves behavior, then apply the final intended Instance update once.

body.code:
-- Bad: Crossing the bridge multiple times inside a loop
for i = 1, 10000 do
    local offset = part.Position + Vector3.new(i, 0, 0)
    part.Position = offset
end

-- Good: Caching and modifying local state, then writing once
local currentPos = part.Position
for i = 1, 10000 do
    currentPos = currentPos + Vector3.new(i, 0, 0)
end
part.Position = currentPos

body.p: This example is a refactoring shape, not a performance promise. Keep the version only if the profiler and player-facing behavior justify it.

gaps.title: Plan-Before-You-Build Module
gaps.why: Roblox developers often jump straight into typing script code without planning their execution loops, leading to structural spaghetti and endless debugging. This module outlines how to design system architectures on paper first.

body.p: A game loop manages the lifecycle of rounds and frames. Before scripting, define the states of your game loop (Intermission, Play, RoundOver) using state machine transitions. Ensure that only one state is active at a time to prevent overlapping timers.

Instead of building a massive list of half-finished weapons and cosmetics (a feature list), build a 'Vertical Slice.' Create one single button, one dropper, and one item. Connect them completely, save the progress, and secure the client-server remote. If this slice works flawlessly, scaling up is trivial.

Plan the shape of your state before touching datastores. Define who owns the state (Server or Client) and what persists. For a tycoon, your state shape should be a flat key-value dictionary to make serialization trivial.

body.h: 4. Concrete Case: Naive vs Boundary-Aware Tycoon
body.p: Compare a naive implementation (where every button Touched connection queries leaderstats and spawns parts directly on the server) against a boundary-aware OOP implementation (where a central Plot class manages purchases via a dedicated Purchase method, validates currency states, and fires local bindable signals for clean UI updates). The naive approach results in severe spaghetti and replication lags, requiring a total system rewrite later.

gaps.title: Studio Fundamentals
gaps.why: Scripters often struggle because they treat Roblox Studio as a basic text editor. Understanding the workspace windows, test runner modes, and diagnostic tools is critical for professional-grade debugging.

body.p: The Explorer lists all live Instances. Keep your hierarchy clean: scripts belong in ServerScriptService, local scripts belong in StarterPlayerScripts or StarterGui, and shared library modules belong in ReplicatedStorage. Use the Properties window to monitor instance states in real-time during Play Solo.

body.h: 2. Testing Modes (Play Solo vs Team Test vs Run)
list: Play Solo: Spawns a local player character and simulates both Client and Server in a single process. Fast but can hide latency issues.
Team Test: Spawns a real multiplayer server container, allowing you to test remote replication and race conditions across multiple client windows.
Run: Simulates the server-side environment in Studio without spawning a player character. Ideal for testing backend loops or auto-saving databases.

body.p: The Output window is your console log. Make sure to enable 'Show Context' in settings. Use breakpoints in the Debugger window to pause execution, inspect local variable values, and step through lines of Luau code step-by-step.

Open the MicroProfiler (Ctrl+F6) to inspect frames in detail. The profiler shows a visual bar chart of engine tasks (rendering, physics solver, scripts). If your script causes frame drops, look for a long orange script bar (representing script execution time) and inspect the call stack to find the exact function responsible.

gaps.title: Ecosystem Literacy
gaps.why: Community libraries can solve recurring architectural problems, but names, maintenance, guarantees, and performance claims change. Learn the problem first, then evaluate a package deliberately.

body.p: As games grow, tracking event connections, parts, and thread lifetimes manually becomes impossible. Community cleanup classes act as central registries to track and clean resources at once. Trove (using trove:Add and trove:Clean), Janitor (using janitor:Add and janitor:Cleanup), and Maid (using maid:GiveTask and maid:DoCleaning) all solve the exact same problem: preventing memory leaks from orphan objects or connections.

A Signal-style module can give local code a `:Connect` / `:Fire` interface. Choose one only when its lifecycle, error behavior, type support, maintenance, and measured need are clear; a package is not automatically faster or safer than a documented engine primitive.

Many Roblox APIs yield (like HttpService or DataStores). The Promise library provides a standard wrapper (Promise.new) to wrap yielding calls, allowing you to chain them with andThen and catch, run multiple network calls in parallel, or cancel a request mid-flight.

Persistence wrappers can help with session ownership, schema defaults, retries, and lifecycle handling, but they are not a substitute for understanding server authority and failure states. Review a library’s current documentation, license, maintenance, and test behavior before making it part of a live game.

gaps.title: Iterate/Playtest Workflow
gaps.why: Fixing bugs by changing random lines of code leads to regressions. A professional scripter uses a structured scientific loop to isolate, profile, and fix issues.

list: 1. Formulate Hypothesis: 'The purchase button lags because it runs a heavy spatial query on every touch.'
2. Playtest & Gather Evidence: Open Studio, trigger the button, and check the Output console or MicroProfiler bars.
3. Change One Thing: Modify the code to cache the spatial search result or add a cooldown.
4. Verify: Run the playtest again to check if the issue is resolved and ensure no other features broke.

body.p: If a bug occurs in a complex tycoon script, copy the subsystem into an empty baseplate first. Verify it behaves correctly in isolation. Once it passes there, integrate it back into the main game. This saves hours of reload and playtest time.

gaps.title: Structuring a Full Game (Architecture)
gaps.why: Transitioning from isolated script files to a production-grade game architecture requires understanding service-controller patterns, packaging managers (Wally), and project synchronization frameworks (Rojo).

body.p: A modular architecture separates backend server logic (Services) from client-side UI and interaction logic (Controllers). A Service runs on the server, reads data stores, and communicates with client-side Controllers via RemoteEvents/RemoteFunctions. Controllers handle player input, update UI frames, and trigger visual effects. Neither should directly access variables of the other side.

list: ReplicatedStorage: Shared packages (Wally), network modules, utilities, and client-server type definitions.
ServerScriptService: Backend logic, database managers, purchase services, and game loops.
ServerStorage: Templates, assets, and databases that the client must never access or download.
StarterPlayerScripts: Client bootstrap controller scripts.

body.p: Wally is a package manager for Roblox. It allows you to define dependencies in a `wally.toml` file (e.g. `Promise = "evaera/promise@4.0.0"`) and download them into a `Packages` folder. Rojo synchronizes your local filesystem to Roblox Studio in real-time. By tracking codebase modules inside a Git repository (like GitHub) and syncing via Rojo, developers bypass the limitations of Studio's built-in script editor, utilizing tools like VS Code, type linters, and branch version control.

gaps.title: Production Workflows
gaps.why: Professional codebases avoid hardcoded configurations and direct access patterns. Designing clean configuration shapes, typed structures, and mock databases is critical for zero-regression development.

body.p: Before saving data to DataStores, construct a clear type layout and default templates in a shared module. Use typed structures to prevent key mismatches or missing properties during runtime serialization.

Use a mock repository for deterministic local tests. Data store calls are server-side network calls; Studio API access is an explicit, risky test setting and should never point a learning place at live player data. Keep real persistence tests in a separately published test experience with a deliberate failure plan.

Instead of hardcoding game constants (like a weapon's damage or speed) in script files, store them as Instance Attributes inside physical objects. This allows designers to balance the game directly from the Properties panel without writing code, and scripts can read them dynamically using `instance:GetAttribute("Damage")`.

gaps.title: Creator Field Note — the first 60 seconds
gaps.why: A game needs a player promise before it needs more systems. This field note turns a vague opening into a small Studio experiment with observable evidence, not a retention claim.

body.p: Use one sentence: `In the first minute, the player will ___ by ___, then understand ___`. Make it specific enough to build. Example: `In the first minute, the player will activate LaunchPad, see an accepted result, then know where to go next.`

list: 0–10 seconds: spawn safely; the first useful object is visible.
10–30 seconds: the player performs one understandable action.
30–60 seconds: the game returns feedback and points to one next goal.

body.h: 3. Test the path without inventing a metric
body.p: Run a short Studio playtest with a person who has not been coached. Record what they tried first, where they paused, what they said they expected, and whether they reached the next goal. One playtest is evidence about that session, not proof of retention, discovery, or market demand.

body.h: 4. Change one hypothesis at a time
body.p: Write `I believe players miss the LaunchPad because ___`. Change one cue, replay the same path, and compare the observation. Keep the result even when it disproves your idea. Do not use pressure patterns, fake urgency, or opaque reward loops to force the outcome.

gaps.title: Creator Field Note — from path to metric
gaps.why: Analytics is a way to find the next question, not a machine that proves why players acted. Instrument one meaningful path only after the server has accepted it, then decide what to investigate next.

body.p: Write the uncertainty before choosing a number: `Where does a new player stop understanding the first-minute path?` A metric is useful only if its result could change your next action. `Track everything` is not a question.

list: Joined the experience.
Saw the first useful cue.
Reached the LaunchPad.
Completed the first server-accepted activation.
Reached the next stated goal.

body.h: 3. Log the accepted event, not a client claim
body.p: For a real onboarding funnel, Roblox event logging runs from a server Script in a published experience — Studio playtesting cannot prove delivery. Use stable step numbers and names, and log only when the server has already accepted the action.

body.code:
-- Server-side: only after the validated LaunchPad action succeeds
local AnalyticsService = game:GetService("AnalyticsService")

if sessionState[player].launches == 1 then
    AnalyticsService:LogOnboardingFunnelStepEvent(
        player,
        4,
        "First accepted LaunchPad activation"
    )
end

body.h: 4. Read a drop as a lead, not a verdict
body.p: If fewer players reach a step, investigate that transition: was the cue visible, was the instruction clear, did a technical problem occur, or did the cohort differ? Compare a defined time window or cohort, change one hypothesis, and keep a guardrail such as error rate or completion of the next goal. Do not claim the change caused the result from one chart alone.

Record `date → question → funnel step → comparison → change → guardrail → result → next action or rollback`. This is how an analytics dashboard becomes a learning loop instead of pressure to chase a number.

gaps.title: Creator Field Note — depth without clutter
gaps.why: Depth is not a pile of features. It is a repeatable player decision that changes with context, has a readable consequence, and can be learned without a spreadsheet.

body.p: Write `When ___ happens, the player chooses between ___ and ___ because ___`. The alternatives must trade something real: safety versus speed, certain reward versus risky reward, immediate progress versus information, or solo control versus cooperation.

body.h: 2. Add variation, not a second tutorial
list: Define the context that changes the choice: map position, opponent behavior, scarce resource, teammate need, or a new goal.
Define feedback: what tells the player their choice mattered right now?
Define failure and recovery: what is lost, what can be learned, and how does the player try again without a punishing restart?

body.p: Do not build a tree of systems. Put the choice, consequence, and recovery into one tiny playable loop. Ask an uncoached tester what they noticed, what they chose, and what they would try differently next time.

In Game Forge, add the choice to Mechanics & Systems and record one uncertainty in the Experiment Ledger. A tester saying `I liked it` is useful feedback, but not proof that the system has long-term depth.

gaps.title: Creator Field Note — economy pacing without pressure
gaps.why: A healthy economy makes progress legible. It maps sources, sinks, and time-to-goal before it ever tries to sell something optional.

head: Question
Write one answer
rows: What action gives the player a resource, and how often?
About how many normal actions reach the next meaningful choice?
What happens after a mistake or a poor choice?
What can be purchased without blocking the core path?

body.p: Example: `I believe the first useful upgrade arrives too late because players leave before they can make a second meaningful choice.` Change one rate or cost, then watch first-session completion and player understanding—not only purchase conversion.

Write down what the game will not do: false scarcity, unclear odds, misleading timers, blocked basic play, or a prompt aimed at a frustrated player. A measurable purchase is not automatically a healthy one.
Use Game Forge’s Economy and Ethical Economy fields for the source/sink map and value promise. Use the Experiment Ledger for the pacing test, its guardrail, and the keep/revise/rollback decision.

action.label: Map your economy in Game Forge →

gaps.title: Creator Field Note — map layout is a player system
gaps.why: A map is not decoration around mechanics. It controls what players see, where choices happen, how risk is telegraphed, and whether a goal can be understood without a tutorial wall.

list: Spawn: what useful object or direction is visible immediately?
Choice: where does the player decide, and what information is present?
Consequence: where do they see the result of that decision?
Recovery: how do they re-enter the loop after a failure or detour?

body.p: Write `I believe players miss ___ because the cue competes with ___.` Change one path cue, sightline, landmark, or interaction distance. Observe the same first-minute route with an uncoached player.

A tighter map, brighter arrow, or shorter route is not universally better. It may improve orientation while harming tension, discovery, social play, or performance. Name the trade-off and choose a guardrail.
Put the route and friction point in Game Forge’s First-session Audit. Log the single spatial change, primary signal, and guardrail in the Experiment Ledger.

gaps.title: Creator Field Note — change one thing, then prove it
gaps.why: Updates become learning only when a team can later answer what changed, for whom, against what comparison, and what it decided to do next.

body.p: Use: `For ___ cohort, changing ___ should affect ___ because ___. I will also watch ___ as a guardrail.` If the result could not change a decision, the metric is probably decoration.

list: Define the starting window or mature cohort and the review date.
Name concurrent changes, acquisition differences, device mix, or missing data.
Change the smallest reversible thing you can reasonably isolate.

body.p: Record the observed result, alternate explanations, and one decision: keep, revise, roll back, or gather more evidence. Do not write `proved` when the evidence only made a hypothesis more or less plausible.
Game Forge’s Experiment Ledger is the source of truth. It keeps the hypothesis, primary signal, guardrail, smallest change, result, and decision together instead of scattering them in chat messages.

honesty: Parallel Luau (multithreading) placed in Track 7, after performance. Judgment, not forced — it only hard-requires the Track 2 event/scheduler model and could be pulled forward. I deferred it because parallelizing before you can profile is usually a mistake.
Raycasting forced after CFrames. The real dependency is Vector3 (position + direction), but there's no standalone Vector3 page, so CFrame is an approximate forcing prerequisite.
Where Users/UserIds belongs. Placed in Track 4 next to remotes, but it has no hard dependency on remotes — it could equally sit in Track 0 as basic DataModel knowledge. Pure judgment.

Input placed in Track 2 rather than after the client–server boundary. Input is client-side, and "input only exists on the client" is really a boundary fact (Track 4); I kept it as an events topic, so the boundary nuance is a forward reference.

The whole Tooling track ordered last. Nothing depends on it, so its position is the weakest in the file — Script Editor / Output / Debugging are useful from Track 1 onward, and you should dip into them immediately rather than wait.

Promoting Type Checking to a Core track instead of a reference. Traditionally treated as optional, but mastering large Roblox codebases requires typed interfaces to eliminate a massive category of type mismatches early.

Placing the Memory Model & GC immediately after metatables instead of at the end of the course. Understanding GC early governs how developers write event bindings and clear cache references, preventing critical leaks early.
